# Basic Transitive Resolution
*or what to Do When Gradle Resolves More Than You Declared*

::: {.scenario}
This scenario demonstrates fundamental concept of transitive dependency resolution. It shows how it automatically discovers and includes dependencies of your direct dependencies.
:::

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.0.0]
    LibA --> LibB[lib-b:1.0.0]
    LibB --> LibC[lib-c:1.0.0]
```



```{.groovy include="/demos/gradle-demo/scenario-1-basic/build.gradle" snippet="s1-deps"}
```

#### Reader Expectation
Declaring `lib-a` automatically includes its transitive dependencies `lib-b` and `lib-c`.

#### Why This Happens
Gradle performs a depth-first traversal of the graph, following every edge in POM or Gradle module metadata files. In the absence of version conflicts, it collects every unique artifact to build the complete classpath.

#### How This Scales in Real Systems
In production projects, this simple chain (A → B → C) is replicated thousands of times. A typical Spring Boot application resolves 200--400 transitive dependencies from a handful of direct declarations. The "happy path" of zero conflicts becomes increasingly rare as the graph grows; diamond dependencies emerge naturally.

#### Deterministic Checks

**Fast check (Gradle):**

```bash
./gradlew dependencies
```

**What to look for:**
A clean tree with no unexpected versions or `omitted` nodes.

**Conclusion:**
The dependency graph is healthy and resolving as expected.

#### Key Takeaway
It resolves the full graph by default.

#### Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 2: Conflict](../scenario-2-conflict/guide.qmd)
